items.q: What is Taylor's theory of scientific management?
items.correct: Workers are motivated primarily by money; efficiency is maximised by breaking tasks into standardised steps, training workers in 'the one best way', and using piece-rate pay.
items.misconception: Taylor said workers are motivated by recognition and teamwork.
items.random_wrong: Taylor invented the assembly line at Ford.
items.why: Students confuse Taylor with Mayo or Maslow. Taylor's view was that workers respond to pay incentives, not social or psychological factors — his theory is famously narrow and mechanistic.

items.q: What are Porter's two main generic strategies for competitive advantage?
items.correct: Cost leadership (being the lowest-cost producer) and differentiation (offering unique value customers will pay more for) — Porter argues firms should choose one or the other.
items.misconception: Porter says businesses should pursue both low cost and differentiation at once.
items.random_wrong: Porter's strategies are about marketing channels.
items.why: Porter's central warning is against being 'stuck in the middle'. Students assume more strategies = better; Porter argues choosing one focus is more effective than trying both.

items.q: What is corporate governance, and how does it differ from CSR?
items.correct: Corporate governance is the system of rules, practices and processes by which a company is directed and controlled — focusing on accountability, board structure and shareholder rights. CSR is about wider social/environmental responsibility.
items.misconception: Corporate governance is the same as corporate social responsibility.
items.random_wrong: Corporate governance is the legal requirement to have a CEO.
items.why: Governance is about how the company is run (board, audit, transparency); CSR is about what the company does for society. Students conflate them — both concern ethics but at different levels.
The answer examiners want is: Create urgency. “Build a coalition” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Six. “Two” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many steps in Kotter's model?
items.why: Exact answer: 8. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
